I agree with what you added but find some of what Greg said questionable. 

Firstly commercial banks are not participating BECAUSE they are price sensitive and the curve is too flat. 

Secondly and far more importantly the big seemingly price insensitive buyer of duration is the real money leveraged investor without balance sheet capacity who buys futures or more importantly receives swaps.  They seem to have infinite demand for duration and he doesn't address them at all.  But weirdly mentions hedge funds and the basis trade.  Hedge funds aren't in any real way "supporting"
The treasury market as they are taking NO interest rate risk.  They are simply taking repo market risk and using balance sheet capacity to arb the basis.  

The real question is why are real money investors continually taking on duration risk via swaps at horrible prices.  (Price insensitive) and what would cause them to slow/stop/or unwind

Good morning.
This headline serves as a reminder that while the yen has seemingly stabilized around 162 per US dollar, the fallout has yet to fully play out.
As an example, Japan faces the risk that this opens the door wide to a round of corporate price hikes.
Should such increases raise new questions about the authorities' policy reaction function, it could then spark a renewed wave of currency weakness.
As discussed before, there is a solution. It lies in a comprehensive and coordinated policy response involving the Prime Minister's office, the Ministry of Finance, and the Bank of Japan.
#economy #markets #japan #yen #boj #currency #fx #inflation #apple

thread_tweets.text:
- Sk Hynix mainly traded in Korea
- $SKHY lists in the US with limited shares, ~2.5% of the float (this is called ADR)
- Even though SK Hynix are the same company, the shares trade independently. 
- Sk Hynix US traded at a 20-50% premium to SK Hynix Korean listing due to limited supply, but lot of US demand. 
-> ADRs allow for conversion between Korea Shares to US Shares, and conversion will start on the 29th
- If you convert 22.5% of the Korean supply to the US, that 20-50% premium is likely to go down 

eg. If there's limited mangos, and someone pays $3 for mangos.

You buy the same mango for $1. You keep buying those $1 mangos and selling for $3 until the mangos pricing converge. That's called arbitrage and what likely traders would do.

tweets.text:
#Silver #Gold #Uranium #Nuclear #Commodities

Global debt keeps climbing, yet few governments have the political appetite for real austerity. History suggests the most likely path is financial repression: higher inflation, lower real interest rates, and gradual currency debasement rather than outright defaults.

That’s a powerful long-term backdrop for precious metals.

🥇 Gold remains the ultimate monetary asset, with central banks continuing to accumulate reserves as they diversify away from fiat currencies.

🥈 Silver offers a rare combination of monetary and industrial demand. A structural supply deficit, electrification, AI-driven grid expansion, solar, and investment demand could all converge in the years ahead.

☢️ Uranium stands to benefit from a different side of the same story. As countries prioritize energy security and reliable baseload power while investment in new supply continues to be limited, nuclear energy becomes increasingly strategic. Years of underinvestment in uranium mining leaves the utilities with a huge fuel problem as it often takes 10-20 years to bring new mines online.

If governments choose inflation over austerity—and history suggests they often do—hard assets and scarce commodities will be among the biggest beneficiaries going forward.

The macro backdrop is becoming increasingly difficult to ignore.

tweets.text:
Imagine spending years building a house, then leaving your family a locked door with no
spare key.

That’s basically what happens with a lot of crypto inheritance today.

The assets are sitting right there, but your family's stuck digging through old notebooks, phones, and cloud folders for a seed phrase they don't fully understand.

One missing detail and years of building is effectively unreachable.

Most people just... avoid thinking about this. I get why, It's morbid and there's been no clean answer.

That’s exactly the part of bron wallet I found genuinely interesting.

Their mobile app is now live, and the inheritance flow is built directly into the wallet.

You choose the beneficiaries in advance, but they can’t see your balances or activity while you’re alive.

If something happens, they can begin a recovery process through pre-selected Guardians, followed by a 48-hour recovery and multi-party checks before access
changes.

The setup avoids passing seed phrases between family group chat, and no single person holding complete control.

Underneath, Bron uses MPC, which splits key control across multiple parties instead of creating one master secret that can be lost or stolen.

It doesn’t replace a legal will, and third-party audits of the inheritance flow are still pending.

But as a wallet feature, this feels like one of the more practical attempts at solving a problem most crypto users avoid thinking about.

Glad to partner with @bronwallet on this one.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Building wealth isn’t the finish line, even though most financial planning focuses on growing assets. The harder question is what happens to that wealth when you’re no longer around to manage it.

For crypto holders, that question has never had a particularly good answer. https://t.co/qFVabVzrfO

That’s exactly the part of bron wallet I found genuinely interesting.

Their mobile app is now live, and the inheritance flow is built directly into the wallet.

You choose the beneficiaries in advance, but they can’t see your balances or activity while you’re alive.

If something happens, they can begin a recovery process through pre-selected Guardians, followed by a 48-hour recovery and multi-party checks before access
changes.

The setup avoids passing seed phrases between family group chat, and no single person holding complete control.

Underneath, Bron uses MPC, which splits key control across multiple parties instead of creating one master secret that can be lost or stolen.

It doesn’t replace a legal will, and third-party audits of the inheritance flow are still pending.

But as a wallet feature, this feels like one of the more practical attempts at solving a problem most crypto users avoid thinking about.

Glad to partner with @bronwallet on this one.

David Sacks: Everything Kathy Hochul Said About Datacenters Was a False Accusation

@DavidSacks: “These data centers become the scapegoat for all of the angst that people have about AI.

Everything she's saying there is a false accusation on datacenters. So let's just go one by one.”

POWER:

So she's saying that they eat up all of the power. Well, yeah, if you connect to the grid without producing more power and you force datacenters to compete with residential ratepayers, then yeah, you could drive up utility prices.

However, if you do what Chamath said and let them build behind the meter, then they bring their own power. And that's what the president has advocated for since the beginning of his administration, is let the AI companies become power companies.

So that is the way to solve the energy problem or the utility problem.

LAND:

Then, she's talking about eating up land. The reality is these datacenters are a model of land use efficiency.

We have a ton of land in this country, obviously.

NOISE POLLUTION:

The supposed noise pollution, that's largely made up. That can be dealt with.

You obviously don't want to put these things right next to a residential area, but create a little bit of distance, and it's fine.

WATER CONSUMPTION:

The whole water consumption thing is largely a hoax.

Modern datacenters recirculate the water.

AIR POLLUTION:

One final thing just on the point that Hochul is making. She said it created a lot of pollution.

Natural gas, which is how most of these data centers are powered, is one of the most clean burning sources of power that we have.

“When you compare economic impact to all these different things, datacenters are honestly one of the best things we could be building as a nation.”

tweets.text: Imagine that virtually everything important going on in your company can be captured as data, and that you can build algorithms to instruct the computer, as you would instruct a person, to analyze that data and use it in the way you agreed it should be used. In that way, you and the computer on your behalf could look at each person and all the people together and provide tailored guidance, just like your GPS provides you guidance by knowing all the traffic patterns and routes. You don't have to make it mandatory to follow that guidance, though you can. Generally speaking, the system operates like a coach. And the coach can learn about its team: Data is collected about what people do so that if they make more insightful moves or less insightful moves, learning will occur and be used to create improvements. Because the thinking behind the algorithms is available to everyone, anyone can assess the quality of the logic and its fairness, and have a hand in shaping it. #principleoftheday

i will elaborate on this a bit from the perspective of an American on what Americans don't understand about East Asia / Southeast Asia:

- for a long time, the common belief among Americans was that East Asia (and especially Southeast Asia) were poor and third world. and they were for a long time (though some developed much earlier than others, like Japan, Taiwan, Singapore, and South Korea)
- this has changed dramatically in the past 2+ decades. most of these countries are far removed from poverty, and even the ones closer to it (in Southeast Asia) are developing very quickly. in those countries especially, the wealth disparity is notable
- and it's true, in many of these countries, there is FAR LOWER street crime in many areas. they are ridiculously, absurdly safe by American or even European standards (especially in most of East Asia). you can walk outside at any time of night on any street and no one will bother you
- the famous Asian hospitality/service standards largely comes from "cheap labor," but it is plentiful, and if you have money, makes living in these societies very different from Western societies. most people talking about "how much nicer it is to live in Asia" are really referring to products of these dynamics
- if you don't already have money or position, societal mobility is NOT good in these societies (and significantly, really bad in some societies)
- BUT all societies globally may now start to suffer this as AI and then robotics come to the forefront and owners of capital dominate
- then Confucian ideals underpinning these societies become an extremely strong point as they naturally promote harmony. it doesn't mean there can't be unrest, but it's different from America where everyone is looking for reasons to fight with one another these days or anything
- my general feeling is that if the US abdicates its influence in East Asia (really a carryover from WW2 redevelopment days), then China will move in very quickly to establish firm regional hegemony
- but i won't claim to understand China. there are some reasons why i haven't been there and won't be able to go in the near future (despite having been to many other places in the region), however, its influence is felt over the entire region in one way or another. Americans have been fed a lot of propaganda about China over the past couple of decades. first that they would collapse "soon" under their own weight. now that they are too far behind on AI to catch up. i can't relay the ground truth, and even if i were on the ground, i'm not sure the truth would be easy to discern
- but i can say the American propaganda on China has been way too shallow and jingoistic in character, and we risk dramatically underestimating a rising, great power we don't really understand
- China has been very good at exporting goods, but not so good at exporting culture in modern times (though was very good in older/ancient times). this is actually its biggest weakness. compare to Japan (where its cultural influence is obvious in businesses all over East Asia) and Korea (with K-pop as a global sensation)
- but it's not so simple. modern Chinese immigrants and sometimes even tourists are often viewed with suspicion/disdain in a lot of these countries, however, in many of them, older Chinese families who came decades or centuries ago are some of the prosperous (and powerful) diasporas in many of these countries. as an outsider, it's a fascinating dichotomy

i'm not done with my travels, and frankly i'm still making sense of it. i'm viewing things through my own lens as an American, too. i suspect people could nitpick or misread some of this for sure, but one thing you can't miss is the feeling of dynamism in these societies. stuff is happening. it feels like most of them are moving forward and seeing better days with people who are excited about the future. that has probably been the most notable thing to observe

having spent a lot of time in East Asia over the past year

i can tell you most Americans don't understand what's happening in East Asia

i will elaborate on this a bit from the perspective of an American on what Americans don't understand about East Asia / Southeast Asia:

- for a long time, the common belief among Americans was that East Asia (and especially Southeast Asia) were poor and third world. and they were for a long time (though some developed much earlier than others, like Japan, Taiwan, Singapore, and South Korea)
- this has changed dramatically in the past 2+ decades. most of these countries are far removed from poverty, and even the ones closer to it (in Southeast Asia) are developing very quickly. in those countries especially, the wealth disparity is notable
- and it's true, in many of these countries, there is FAR LOWER street crime in many areas. they are ridiculously, absurdly safe by American or even European standards (especially in most of East Asia). you can walk outside at any time of night on any street and no one will bother you
- the famous Asian hospitality/service standards largely comes from "cheap labor," but it is plentiful, and if you have money, makes living in these societies very different from Western societies. most people talking about "how much nicer it is to live in Asia" are really referring to products of these dynamics
- if you don't already have money or position, societal mobility is NOT good in these societies (and significantly, really bad in some societies)
- BUT all societies globally may now start to suffer this as AI and then robotics come to the forefront and owners of capital dominate
- then Confucian ideals underpinning these societies become an extremely strong point as they naturally promote harmony. it doesn't mean there can't be unrest, but it's different from America where everyone is looking for reasons to fight with one another these days or anything
- my general feeling is that if the US abdicates its influence in East Asia (really a carryover from WW2 redevelopment days), then China will move in very quickly to establish firm regional hegemony
- but i won't claim to understand China. there are some reasons why i haven't been there and won't be able to go in the near future (despite having been to many other places in the region), however, its influence is felt over the entire region in one way or another. Americans have been fed a lot of propaganda about China over the past couple of decades. first that they would collapse "soon" under their own weight. now that they are too far behind on AI to catch up. i can't relay the ground truth, and even if i were on the ground, i'm not sure the truth would be easy to discern

i'm not done with my travels, and frankly i'm still making sense of it. i'm viewing things through my own lens as an American, too. i suspect people could nitpick or misread some of this for sure, but one thing you can't miss is the feeling of dynamism in these societies. stuff is happening. it feels like most of them are moving forward and seeing better days with people who are excited about the future. that has probably been the most notable thing to observe

The next period of yield farming will be "inference farming"

People used to farm perps because perps platforms make a lot of money and accrue them back to token holders (i.e. Hyperliquid)

Now, we're seeing the same thing starting to happen with decentralized inference platforms

Open models finally reach frontier status at a fraction of frontier cost (i.e. Kimi K3). This will bring volume to decentralized inference players who can host & sell the inference at 30%+ cheaper vs centralized players

Farmers can simply farm the inference by renting GPUs online and contribute to the network.

A few picks to start from: Dolphin AI, SN53 Engy, and AntSeed AI.

tweets.text:
I think it’ll be a fight between:

- Objectiveness and publicly verifiable evidences (ie smart contract invariant broken; funds stolen) 

- Subjectivity and confidence intervals (ie we believe with 69% certainty funds belong to DPRK) 

Former is desirable, latter is a big slippery slope. Of course even if a hack is publicly verifiable, if it’s not caught early then it can become very subjective quickly including who the real victims are.
